context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk rn, PS6 is frying my brain 😭 maybe the graph coloring stuff, but I’m kinda lost on all of it.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — graph coloring can feel like a lot at first! Let's break it down together. Which problem feels most pressing right now: Problem 1 (the failed induction), Problem 2 (the Grötzsch graph coloring), or would you rather start with the stable matching problems (3 or 4)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing overwhelm but hasn't committed to a specific problem yet. I should orient them toward the assignment and help them pick a starting point, without giving any content away. I'll acknowledge their frustration briefly and ask them to pick a problem to start with, steering them toward engagement.

exchanges.student: uh maybe Problem 2? tbh I don’t even get what they want in part (b).
exchanges.tutor: Good choice! Before we dig in, can you try restating what part (b) is asking in your own words? Just read it carefully and tell me what you think the goal is.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to work on Problem 2(b) but says they don't understand what's being asked. Rather than explaining it for them, I should use a Socratic question to get them to re-read and parse the problem statement themselves. I'll ask them to rephrase what the problem is asking in their own words, which will help clarify their understanding without me doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: i think it’s saying “if 3-coloring exists, it has to look basically like that picture,” but idk how I’m supposed to prove a picture fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's exactly the right intuition! So now think about this: if you only have 3 colors, what can you say about how the inner 5-cycle (v0 through v4) must be colored?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly understood the high-level goal of part (b), which is good progress. Now I should help them think about what 'proving a picture' actually means mathematically - i.e., what constraints does a 3-coloring impose on the graph structure. I'll ask a targeted question to move them toward thinking about the inner 5-cycle specifically.

exchanges.student: that’s where I’m stuck tho, like cycles/colors all blur together rn and I kinda need the big picture first.
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough. Let's go back to basics: what's the fundamental rule that any valid graph coloring must satisfy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing confusion about cycles and coloring. I should help them zoom out to the most basic constraint before tackling the 5-cycle specifically. A simple Socratic question about what graph coloring requires should ground them without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: adjacent vertices need diff colors, but then I still don’t see how that turns into the whole forced pattern tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Good. Now, can you try to 2-color a 5-cycle (just 5 vertices in a loop) using only 2 colors, following that rule? What happens?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student knows the basic coloring rule but can't yet connect it to the forced pattern. I should guide them to think about odd cycles specifically - a 5-cycle is an odd cycle, which has a special property with respect to 2-coloring. This is the key insight that forces the pattern. I'll ask a targeted question about what happens when you try to color a 5-cycle with only 2 colors.

exchanges.student: i end up stuck at the end cuz the last one touches the first, so 2 colors fail on odd cycles i guess.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right! So if the 5-cycle needs at least 3 colors, and you only have 3 total — what does that tell you about how those 3 colors must be distributed around the cycle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that odd cycles can't be 2-colored. This is the key insight. Now I should push them to connect this to the 3-coloring of the 5-cycle in the Grötzsch graph - specifically, what does this imply about how many colors the 5-cycle must use, and what pattern does that force?

exchanges.student: ig one color has to get reused? but honestly I’m not seeing the full chain, can u just explain the structure of the proof cuz I’m super lost.
exchanges.tutor: I won't lay out the proof for you, but you're closer than you think! You said one color gets reused — on a 5-cycle with 3 colors, how many times exactly does that one repeated color appear? And can those repeated vertices be adjacent?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the proof structure, which would essentially give away the solution. I should not do this. However, the student is showing some signs of frustration and going in circles a bit. I should keep them on track with a more targeted Socratic question that moves them forward without giving the structure away. Their last answer ('one color has to get reused') is actually on the right track - I should push them to be more precise about what 'reused' means on a 5-cycle with 3 colors.

exchanges.student: maybe twice, and not adjacent? ngl I’m still guessing.
exchanges.tutor: That's not a guess — that's correct! One color appears twice on non-adjacent vertices, and the other two colors each appear once or twice filling in the rest. Now, the Grötzsch graph also has a central vertex w connected to all of v0–v4. How does w's color interact with whatever colors appear on the 5-cycle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is guessing but the answer is actually correct - on a 5-cycle with 3 colors, one color appears exactly twice on non-adjacent vertices. I should affirm this and push them to think about what this means for the specific structure of the Grötzsch graph, connecting it to the partial coloring in the figure.

exchanges.student: if w touches all of them then w can’t use any color that shows up on the cycle… wait so doesn’t that force 4 colors already??
exchanges.tutor: Interesting leap — hold onto that thought, it might be very useful for part (c)! But for part (b), we're assuming a 3-coloring *does* exist and showing it must look like the picture. So if w connects to all of v0–v4, and w gets one of the 3 colors, what does that constrain about the colors on the 5-cycle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an exciting leap but it's actually incorrect - the 5-cycle uses all 3 colors, so w can't avoid any of them, which would indeed force a 4th color. But wait - this is actually a valid insight for part (c), not part (b)! Part (b) is about showing the coloring must match a specific partial pattern if 3 colors are used. I should gently correct the direction - this insight is valuable but for part (c). For part (b), we need to think about what the forced pattern looks like assuming a 3-coloring exists. I should redirect them.

exchanges.student: ok now I’m extra confused lol, can u just tell me what to focus on in part (b) vs part (c)?
exchanges.tutor: Sure — part (b) assumes a 3-coloring exists and asks you to show it must look like the picture. Part (c) asks you to prove no 3-coloring can exist at all. Part (b) is a stepping stone to part (c). So for now, stay in part (b): given w is one color, what colors are left for the 5-cycle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to clarify the distinction between parts (b) and (c), which is a legitimate clarification question rather than asking for the answer. I can briefly clarify the logical structure of the two parts without giving away any proof content. This will help orient them without doing the work for them.

deductions.reason: In Turn 4, the student explicitly says they need 'the big picture first' and are confused about how the coloring rule leads to a forced pattern. Rather than asking a diagnostic question to identify exactly where the student's understanding breaks down, the tutor jumps straight to a new sub-question ('can you try to 2-color a 5-cycle'). While this is a reasonable scaffolding move, it bypasses diagnosing what specifically the student doesn't understand about the connection between the coloring rule and the forced pattern.

The tutor provides no method-level feedback on the student's reasoning process throughout the conversation. When the student makes progress (e.g., Turn 6, Turn 8), the tutor affirms correctness ('Exactly right!', 'That's not a guess — that's correct!') but never comments on the student's thinking approach, how to structure a proof by forced coloring, or what a good proof strategy looks like for this type of problem.

By Turn 10, the student is still confused about the distinction between parts (b) and (c), and the tutor has been cycling on the same sub-question about w's color and the 5-cycle across Turns 8, 9, and 10 without meaningfully advancing or synthesizing. The student's confusion in Turn 10 ('now I'm extra confused') signals that the repeated questioning pattern is not working, yet the tutor continues the same approach rather than reframing or summarizing what has been established.

grade.overview: The tutor does a solid job maintaining a Socratic approach throughout, never giving away the proof structure or doing the reasoning for the student. The student is guided step-by-step through the 5-cycle coloring logic. However, the tutor fails to provide any meta-level feedback on the student's reasoning process, and by the end of the conversation the student is more confused than before (Turn 10), partly because the tutor cycles on the same w-and-5-cycle question across three turns without reframing or synthesizing. The 1.2.B.a deduction is applied with some uncertainty since the tutor's pivot to the 2-coloring exercise is a reasonable scaffolding move, but it does skip explicit diagnosis.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully preserved: the tutor never gives away the proof, always asks the student to reason first, and declines to explain the structure when asked directly (Turn 7). 1.2 loses 3 points because in Turn 4 the tutor skips diagnosing the student's specific confusion and jumps to a new sub-exercise. 1.3 loses 2 points because the tutor only affirms correctness without ever commenting on proof strategy or reasoning method. 2.1 loses 4 points because by Turns 8–10 the student is increasingly confused and the tutor repeats the same question type (what does w's color constrain?) without reframing, summarizing, or switching approach. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions: the tutor stays on-task, maintains appropriate tone, and keeps responses concise and clear.
